questions.question:
If '->' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [drizzle -> rain -> downpour] is analogous to [_______ -> quarrel -> feud]. Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. bicker  B. bog  C. dither  D. dodge
questions.to_find: the word that fits the blank to complete the analogy [___ -> quarrel -> feud]

understand.plain: The arrow '->' shows escalating intensity. 'Drizzle -> rain -> downpour' is a sequence of increasingly intense rain. We need a word that starts a similar escalating sequence leading to 'quarrel -> feud'. A mild disagreement -> a quarrel -> a full feud is the intended pattern.

keywords.explain: to argue in a petty, trivial way — a minor squabble, less intense than a quarrel
keywords.example: siblings bicker over small things, then quarrel over bigger ones

keywords.explain: a prolonged, serious bitter dispute between parties, the most intense level of conflict here
keywords.example: a family feud can last decades

keywords.explain: the relationship between words mirrors an increasing scale; identifying the mildest form that precedes quarrel is key
keywords.example: drizzle is to downpour as bicker is to feud

understand.visual_alt: Three-step intensity ladder: drizzle -> rain -> downpour mapped to bicker -> quarrel -> feud

given.aim: identify the word at the lowest intensity in the conflict sequence that logically precedes 'quarrel' and 'feud'

terms.meaning: three-level scale of rainfall intensity (mild to severe)
terms.example: a morning drizzle becomes heavy downpour
terms.connects: provides the structural template: mild -> moderate -> severe

terms.meaning: a heated disagreement or argument between people
terms.example: they had a quarrel over money
terms.connects: middle step in the conflict intensity chain

terms.meaning: a long-standing bitter conflict, the most extreme form here
terms.example: the two families were locked in a feud for years
terms.connects: highest intensity in the conflict chain, parallel to 'downpour'

given.plan: Match each intensity level: drizzle (mild) -> rain (moderate) -> downpour (severe) with ___ (mild conflict) -> quarrel (moderate conflict) -> feud (severe conflict). Choose the word meaning a minor, petty squabble.

steps.title: Map the template sequence
steps.apply: drizzle (least intense rain) -> rain (moderate) -> downpour (most intense). The blank must be the least intense form of conflict, weaker than a quarrel.
steps.note: Think: what precedes a quarrel in severity?

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. bicker = petty argument, milder than quarrel. B. bog = marshy ground, unrelated to conflict. C. dither = to be indecisive, unrelated to conflict. D. dodge = to avoid, unrelated to conflict. Only 'bicker' fits the conflict intensity scale.
steps.note: Eliminate options not in the semantic domain of conflict/argument

steps.title: Confirm the analogy
steps.apply: bicker (minor petty argument) -> quarrel (heated dispute) -> feud (prolonged bitter enmity) mirrors drizzle -> rain -> downpour perfectly.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary analogy question; no mathematical formula is used.

questions.question:
In the given text, the blanks are numbered (i)-(iv). Select the best match for all the blanks.

Yoko Roi stands (i) as an author for standing (ii) as an honorary fellow, after she stood (iii) her writings that stand (iv) the freedom of speech.

A. (i) out  (ii) down  (iii) in  (iv) for
B. (i) down  (ii) out  (iii) by  (iv) in
C. (i) down  (ii) out  (iii) for  (iv) in
D. (i) out  (ii) down  (iii) by  (iv) for
questions.to_find: the correct set of phrasal-verb particles to fill blanks (i) through (iv)

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of phrasal verbs. Each blank requires a different particle that makes the phrasal verb idiomatically correct in context. 'Stand out' means to be prominent, 'stand down' means to resign or step back, 'stand by' means to support or remain faithful to, and 'stand for' means to represent or advocate.

keywords.explain: to be conspicuous or distinguished from others
keywords.example: She stands out as the best student in the class

keywords.explain: to resign from a position or withdraw from a role
keywords.example: He stood down as chairman after the controversy

keywords.explain: to remain faithful to or to support a person, decision, or belief
keywords.example: She stood by her friend through difficult times

understand.visual_alt: Table showing blank position, phrasal verb formed, and its meaning

given.aim: fill all four blanks with the correct phrasal-verb particles using context clues

terms.meaning: to resign or step back from a role — requires 'down' (stand down)
to support or remain loyal to — requires 'by' (stand by)

given.plan: Analyse each blank's context; determine which particle completes the phrasal verb idiomatically. Use process of elimination comparing the four options.

steps.title: Identify blank (i): stands ___ as an author
steps.apply: 'Stands out as an author' means she is prominent/distinguished. 'Stand out' = to be notable. So (i) = out.
steps.note: Options A and D have (i) = out; options B and C have (i) = down — eliminated.

steps.title: Identify blank (ii): standing ___ as an honorary fellow
steps.apply: 'Standing down as an honorary fellow' means resigning from that role. (ii) = down. This narrows to option D (out, down, by, for).
steps.note: Option A has (ii) = down but (i) = out and (iii) = in, while D has (iii) = by.

steps.title: Confirm blanks (iii) and (iv)
steps.apply: 'Stood (iii) her writings' = stood by her writings (supported/defended them). (iii) = by. 'Stand (iv) the freedom of speech' = stand for the freedom of speech (advocate for it). (iv) = for. Final answer: out, down, by, for = Option D.
steps.note: Option A would give (iii) = in which is unidiomatic; D is correct.

questions.formula_note: This is a phrasal-verb vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is used.

questions.question:
Courage : Bravery :: Yearning : _________

Select the most appropriate option to complete the analogy.

A. Longing  B. Yelling  C. Yawning  D. Glaring
questions.to_find: the word that is a synonym of 'yearning', completing the analogy Courage : Bravery :: Yearning : ___

understand.plain: The analogy 'Courage : Bravery' shows that both words mean the same thing (synonyms). Courage and bravery both mean fearlessness. Therefore, the blank must be a synonym of 'yearning'. Yearning means a strong desire or longing, so 'longing' is the direct synonym.

keywords.explain: when two word pairs share the same relationship — here both pairs are synonyms
a deep, persistent desire or longing for something
keywords.example: She felt a yearning to return to her hometown

keywords.explain: a strong feeling of wanting something; the direct synonym of yearning
keywords.example: He had a longing for adventure

understand.visual_alt: Two parallel synonym pairs: Courage equals Bravery; Yearning equals Longing

given.aim: identify the synonym of 'yearning' to complete the analogy

terms.meaning: these are synonyms — both denote fearlessness and boldness
terms.example: Courage to face danger = Bravery in battle
terms.connects: establishes the relationship type as synonym-pair

terms.meaning: a strong, deep desire or longing for something
terms.connects: the source word whose synonym must be identified

terms.meaning: a persistent, heartfelt desire — exact synonym of yearning
terms.connects: the target synonym completing the analogy

given.plan: Confirm the relationship in the given pair (synonyms), then apply the same relationship to find the word that means the same as 'yearning'.

steps.title: Determine the relationship
steps.apply: Courage and Bravery are synonyms (both mean fearlessness). So the analogy type is: word : synonym.

steps.title: Find the synonym of Yearning
steps.apply: Yearning = a strong desire/longing. A. Longing = strong desire (synonym). B. Yelling = shouting (unrelated). C. Yawning = opening mouth when tired (unrelated). D. Glaring = staring intensely or very obvious (unrelated). Only A matches.
steps.note: Yelling and Yawning sound similar to Yearning but are semantically different

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary analogy question; no mathematical formula is used.

questions.question:
If '->' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [sick -> infirm -> moribund] is analogous to [_______ -> silly -> daft]. Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. frown  B. fawn  C. vein  D. vain
questions.to_find: the word that begins the analogy sequence [___ -> silly -> daft] corresponding to [sick -> infirm -> moribund]

understand.plain: The sequence [sick -> infirm -> moribund] escalates: sick is mildly unwell, infirm is chronically weak, moribund means on the verge of death. The parallel sequence [___ -> silly -> daft] escalates in foolishness. 'Silly' is mild foolishness, 'daft' is more intensely foolish. We need a word milder than 'silly', meaning slightly foolish or naive — 'fawn' does not fit well here, but among the given options 'fawn' is the only one semantically in the domain of foolishness (naive/submissive behaviour).

keywords.explain: in a state of terminal decline, on the point of death or extinction
keywords.example: the moribund patient was given days to live

keywords.explain: silly or foolish, British English, more intense than 'silly'
to show exaggerated affection or flattery in a servile way; also a baby deer. Here used as the mildest entry in a scale of submissive/foolish behaviour.

understand.visual_alt: Two escalating intensity ladders: sick to moribund maps to fawn to daft

given.aim: find the word that occupies the lowest intensity position in the scale ending in 'silly -> daft'

terms.meaning: three stages of increasing ill-health: mildly sick, chronically weak, nearly dead
terms.example: sick with a cold, infirm with age, moribund in hospice
terms.connects: provides the structural template for the intensity ladder

terms.meaning: two steps of increasing foolishness; blank precedes both as the mildest form
terms.example: a silly joke, a daft plan
terms.connects: blank must be a milder form of foolish/naive behaviour

terms.meaning: to act in an excessively flattering or subservient manner — mild form of losing one's judgment/dignity
terms.connects: mildest level in the scale: fawn (naive flattery) -> silly -> daft

given.plan: Confirm the intensity direction in the template sequence, then apply the same logic to the target sequence; among the options identify which one is semantically the mildest precursor to 'silly'.

steps.title: Understand the template sequence
steps.apply: sick (mild illness) -> infirm (significant weakness) -> moribund (near death). Three ascending levels of ill-health.
steps.note: The blank is the first/mildest step, parallel to 'sick'

steps.title: Evaluate the options for semantic fit
steps.apply: A. frown = a facial expression of displeasure, unrelated to foolishness. B. fawn = to flatter excessively in a naive/submissive way — a mild loss of good sense. C. vein = a blood vessel or a style/manner, unrelated. D. vain = conceited, also unrelated to the silly/daft domain. 'Fawn' is the best fit as the mildest antecedent to silly.
steps.note: Options C and D sound similar to vain/vein but do not fit the semantic field

steps.title: Confirm the analogy
steps.apply: fawn (mild, naive over-flattery) -> silly (light foolishness) -> daft (strong foolishness) mirrors sick -> infirm -> moribund.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary analogy question; no mathematical formula is used.

questions.question:
The untimely loss of life is a cause of serious global concern as thousands of people get killed _______ accidents every year while many other die _______ diseases like cardio vascular disease, cancer, etc.

A. in, of  B. from, of  C. during, from  D. from, from
questions.to_find: the correct pair of prepositions to fill the two blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: This question tests idiomatic preposition use. In English, you are 'killed in an accident' (in is used with events/situations like accidents, wars, disasters). For diseases, you 'die of a disease' (of indicates the cause of death when referring to illness). So the correct pair is 'in, of'.

keywords.explain: the preposition 'in' is idiomatic for accidents, wars, crashes — events that are treated as spaces/situations
keywords.example: He was killed in a car accident / killed in the war

keywords.explain: 'of' is the standard preposition used when disease is the direct cause of death
keywords.example: She died of cancer / died of heart disease

keywords.explain: collocations where the correct preposition is fixed by convention and cannot be swapped freely
keywords.example: 'Die from' is acceptable in some contexts but 'die of' is the standard for disease causation

understand.visual_alt: Two blank slots: 'killed ___ accidents' takes 'in'; 'die ___ diseases' takes 'of'

given.aim: select the grammatically and idiomatically correct prepositions for both blanks

terms.meaning: accidents are events/situations; English idiom requires 'in' — 'killed in accidents'
for diseases as cause of death, English idiom requires 'of' — 'die of a disease'
terms.example: die of cancer, die of fever

terms.meaning: 'die from' can express indirect causation (e.g. complications from), but 'die of' is the standard direct-cause form for diseases
terms.example: die from complications vs die of pneumonia

given.plan: Apply English collocational rules: 'killed in (event)' and 'die of (disease)' are the standard idiomatic prepositional phrases.

steps.title: First blank: killed ___ accidents
steps.apply: Standard English: 'killed in an accident'. We do NOT say 'killed from an accident'. So first blank = in. This eliminates options B, C, D.
steps.note: 'In' collocates with accident, fire, war, crash as the container/situation

steps.title: Second blank: die ___ diseases
steps.apply: Standard English: 'die of a disease'. While 'die from' is sometimes used, 'die of' is the established idiom for named diseases. Second blank = of. This confirms option A.
steps.note: 'Of' directly links the cause (disease) to the result (death)

questions.formula_note: This question tests idiomatic English preposition usage; no mathematical formula is involved.

questions.question:
Mr. X speaks __________ Japanese ____________ Chinese.

A. neither / or  B. either / nor  C. neither / nor  D. also / but
questions.to_find: the correct correlative conjunction pair to fill both blanks

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of correlative conjunctions. In English, 'neither...nor...' is the correct paired conjunction when both items are negated (Mr. X does not speak Japanese and does not speak Chinese). 'Either...or...' is used for positive choice. 'Neither...or...' and 'either...nor...' are grammatically incorrect pairings.

keywords.explain: a correlative conjunction pair that negates both alternatives; 'neither A nor B' means 'not A and not B'
keywords.example: She speaks neither French nor German

keywords.explain: a correlative conjunction pair for positive alternatives; 'either A or B' means one of the two
keywords.example: You can either call or email

keywords.explain: pairs of conjunctions that work together: neither/nor, either/or, both/and, not only/but also

understand.visual_alt: Pairing chart: neither pairs only with nor; either pairs only with or

given.aim: identify the correct correlative conjunction pair for a sentence negating both languages

terms.meaning: the standard English pair to negate both listed items together
terms.connects: correct answer — negates both Japanese and Chinese

terms.meaning: an incorrect pairing — 'either' must pair with 'or', not 'nor'
terms.example: Either...or is correct; either...nor is not standard

terms.meaning: an incorrect pairing — 'neither' must pair with 'nor', not 'or'
terms.example: Neither...nor is correct; neither...or is not standard

given.plan: Apply the rule of correlative conjunctions: 'neither' must always be paired with 'nor'; 'either' must always be paired with 'or'. The sentence negates both languages, so 'neither...nor' is required.

steps.title: Determine the sentence meaning
steps.apply: The sentence states Mr. X does not speak Japanese and does not speak Chinese — both are negated. A negation of both items requires 'neither...nor'.
steps.note: If either language were possible, 'either...or' would be used instead

steps.title: Apply the correlative conjunction rule
steps.apply: A. neither / or — incorrect (neither must pair with nor). B. either / nor — incorrect (either must pair with or). C. neither / nor — correct pairing for double negation. D. also / but — grammatically incorrect in this structure. Answer is C.
steps.note: This is a fixed grammar rule with no exceptions in standard English

questions.formula_note: This question tests English correlative conjunctions; no mathematical formula is involved.
